Off-Grid Datacenter LCOE

The least-cost mix of solar, wind, battery and gas backup to run an always-on, off-grid datacenter on mostly-renewable power — and the year going renewable beats burning gas, across the US and Europe.

▶  View the source code & full methodology on GitHub

Read this first. This is a stylised techno-economic model: trust the directional comparisons, not absolute numbers to better than ~±20–30%. The headline runs on synthetic (not measured) weather and a single generation site by default; both can be replaced with real reanalysis and multi-site portfolios. All numbers below are generated directly from the model's exported results.

The question: how dirty does an AI datacenter have to be?

The boom in AI datacenters has come with a worry that is now a headline in its own right — that they are an environmental problem, that their soaring electricity demand simply means a surge in carbon emissions. This model exists to test, with numbers, how justified that worry is. The real question is not whether a datacenter draws a lot of power — it does — but whether that power can be clean, and what it costs to make it so.

The natural way to clean up a datacenter is to build it together with its own dedicated generation — solar, wind and batteries, off-grid or behind the meter. The catch is that a datacenter is a firm (always-on) load: it needs power every hour of every day, while sun and wind do not. The sun sets; the wind can drop for days at a time (a Dunkelflaute). So the environmental question comes down to the backstop that covers those gaps: do you fall back on a gas turbine, which emits, or on a clean firm option — green hydrogen, a small nuclear reactor, or enough storage — and how much does each cost? Squeezing the last emissions out, from ~90% renewable to a true 100%, is where the bill climbs, because the rarest dark, windless hours dominate it.

This model works out, transparently, the least-cost mix of solar, wind, battery and backup for such a plant, and the resulting delivered cost — its levelised cost of energy (LCOE: the all-in price per MWh of datacenter load once capital, fuel and maintenance are spread over the plant's life) — for the United States and Europe, every year from 2025 to 2040. So it answers the environmental question directly: how much of the year a datacenter can run on renewables; what a clean datacenter costs versus simply burning gas, and when clean becomes the cheaper choice rather than just the greener one; and whether a fully zero-carbon, always-on datacenter is achievable today — and at what premium. The short answer, worked out below: a datacenter need not be dirty. Running fully zero-carbon already costs only a modest premium over gas in the cheap-gas US — and in carbon-priced Europe it is increasingly the cheaper option — with that premium shrinking every year as solar, batteries and electrolysers get cheaper.

Key findings

  • Europe — renewables already win at moderate shares. Carbon-priced, expensive EU gas (rising from $114 to $163/MWh) means a firm 70%-renewable build reaches parity ~2025, and even a 90%-renewable one by ~2030.
  • US — cheap gas is a moat at high renewable shares. Untaxed ~$46/MWh gas keeps a 90%-renewable build off parity beyond 2040 (US 90% crossover >2040); only moderate-share builds cross, in the mid-to-late 2030s.
  • A firm battery-only system tops out near ~94% renewable. Multi-day Dunkelflaute neither sun nor wind covers, and battery power can't bridge days — the last few percent always fall to gas. Going higher needs long-duration storage or hydrogen.
  • A fully gas-free, zero-carbon datacenter is feasible. A co-optimised solar+wind+battery+green-hydrogen build delivers $149→$101/MWh in the EU, crossing below gas around 2030.
  • Where you build dominates the cost. Across a poor→good site in a region the 90%-renewable delivered cost spans tens of $/MWh (the shaded bands in the figures); a multi-site portfolio softens the multi-day lulls and cuts high-renewable cost materially.
  • Off-grid is itself a premium. Staying on the grid with a renewable-energy contract sits below the off-grid high-renewable optimum in both regions — off-grid buys siting independence, at a cost.

The renewable target is the minimum share of the datacenter's yearly energy that must come from solar + wind + battery (the rest is covered by gas). Firm (always-on) workload; gas backup sized to 100% of load. "Crossover" = the first year the build's delivered cost drops below the gas baseline.

Cost trajectories

Across geographies

The same firm off-grid build, now computed at each of seven large EU countries and seven US states (the biggest data-center markets in each region) on real ERA5 reanalysis weather (2015–2025, 11 years) — one grid point per location, every real year a dispatch sample (so the curves carry real interannual variability: dark/calm years included). Within a region only the renewable resource differs — gas, carbon and technology costs are the region default — so this isolates how much where you build, and whether you build a wind park, move the cost.

Is a wind park worth building? (gas-backed)

Solar panels are quick to permit; a wind park is a far bigger siting undertaking, so it is worth asking where one actually pays off. This is a fair test: both builds are optimised to the same renewable target at each site — the most a solar + battery + gas system can reach without wind (~55–68%, shown in each panel) — and the wind build is simply allowed to add a wind park only if it lowers cost. Because building no wind is always an option, the blue line is never above the orange no-wind line: it dips below where wind genuinely competes (the windy markets — the United Kingdom; Texas, Iowa) and all but merges with it where wind is too weak to bother (Arizona, California, Italy, ~2% capacity factor — the optimiser builds almost none, so by the mid-2030s the lines coincide). The grey dashed line is the gas baseline; the purple dash-dot line a small modular (nuclear) reactor. Pushing renewables beyond this no-wind ceiling needs either a wind park where the wind is good, or storage/hydrogen — that is the next two figures. (The reactor is competitive in carbon-priced Europe; in the cheap-gas US the renewable+gas builds sit well below it.)

StateRegionSolar CFWind CFTargetNo wind 2035Wind-optional 2035Wind saves

Real hourly ERA5 at one point per location (2015–2025); solar capacity factor from horizontal irradiance ×1.25; region-default gas, carbon and technology costs. Each site's solar and wind LCOE is re-anchored from the model's calibration capacity factor to that site's real CF, so a low-wind site correctly pays more per MWh for wind (and the optimiser builds none where it is not competitive) and a sunny site pays less for solar. Reduced optimiser fidelity (directional to ~±15% in level — the cross-site ranking and the wind gap are the robust messages). Per-state figures are in figs/locations_re/.

Fully zero-carbon, self-made hydrogen: no wind vs with wind

To drop gas entirely, replace the backstop with green hydrogen made from surplus renewables (the small residual bought from the market). These two builds are zero-carbon by construction — solar + battery + hydrogen (no wind) and solar + wind + battery + hydrogen — on the same 2015–2025 weather. Again the gap between the lines is what a wind park buys: where wind is strong it sits well below the no-wind line (the United Kingdom saves ~$53/MWh, Sweden ~$39, Iowa and Poland ~$30); where wind is scarce — Arizona, California and Italy, all around a 2% capacity factor — the optimiser builds essentially no wind, so the two lines coincide (wind saves ~$0) and the easier-to-permit no-wind build is the one to pick. The grey dashed line is the region gas baseline (it emits); the purple dash-dot line is a small modular (nuclear) reactor — the other firm zero-carbon option — which here is often competitive with the hydrogen build, especially in carbon-priced Europe.

Real hourly ERA5 at one point per location (2015–2025); solar capacity factor from horizontal irradiance ×1.25; region-default carbon and technology costs. Each site's solar and wind LCOE is re-anchored from the calibration capacity factor to that site's real CF, so the optimiser builds wind only where it is competitive (≈0 in the ~2%-CF sites). Reduced optimiser fidelity. Per-state figures are in figs/locations_h2/.

Do you even need a wind park?

Solar panels are modular and quick to permit; a wind park is a far bigger siting and permitting undertaking. So it is worth asking what dropping wind entirely costs. This compares a solar + wind + battery build against solar + battery only (both firm, with gas backup) in 2035.

There is a hard wall. A solar + battery + gas system tops out near ~68% renewable — nights and multi-day cloud always fall to gas, and a battery cannot shift energy across days — whereas adding wind reaches ~94%. Below the wall, solar-only is only modestly pricier in the US (strong solar) and a clearer premium in Europe (weak winter sun). Bottom line: for a moderate renewable target, solar + battery alone is a reasonable, much-easier-to-build choice; pushing to high renewable fractions genuinely needs wind (or long-duration storage / hydrogen).

… and what about solar + battery + hydrogen (no wind)?

To go fully zero-carbon without a wind park, replace the gas backstop with green hydrogen. All the hydrogen options below are green (zero-carbon) — they differ only in how you get the H₂. Making it yourself — a solar-heavy build plus an electrolyser that turns surplus renewables into H₂, buying only a few percent from the market — brings a wind-free zero-carbon datacenter to ~$117/MWh (US) / $130 (EU) by 2035. Buying it all on the market instead (no electrolyser) is far dearer ($171 / $183). And the self-made wind-free build is only ~$12–17 above the same self-made build with a wind park added ($102 / $110) — so the wind park, not the hydrogen, is the smaller lever here. In Europe that with-wind build is actually the cheapest option of all, since carbon-priced gas is dearer.

In the chart, the three green bars are the same green hydrogen; the only differences are whether there is a wind park and whether the H₂ is self-made or bought.

Where in Europe should you build?

The same model can rank candidate locations by the cheapest 24/7 carbon-free delivered power, letting each site use its best clean resource: sunny/windy sites build the gas-free solar + wind + battery + green-hydrogen system; sites with firm zero-carbon resources — geothermal (Iceland) or abundant hydro (Norway, Sweden, the Alps) — simply run on that.

Firm clean baseload wins decisively. Nordic/Alpine hydro (~$46/MWh) and Icelandic geothermal (~$58) beat every build-it-yourself sun-and-wind site and sit far below gas. Among sun + wind sites the Canary Islands lead (steady trade winds + strong sun), then windy Jutland; cloudy/calm markets like Germany trail. (2030, firm · real ERA5 weather.)

LocationCheapest clean resource2030 $/MWh
Norway (hydro)hydro (firm)$46
Sweden (Norrland hydro)hydro (firm)$46
Austrian Alps (hydro)hydro (firm)$46
Iceland (geothermal)geothermal (firm)$63
Canary Is. (Lanzarote)solar + wind + battery + green-H₂$104
Jutland (Denmark)solar + wind + battery + green-H₂$114
Tarifa (Gibraltar Strait)solar + wind + battery + green-H₂$120
S. Portugal (Sines)solar + wind + battery + green-H₂$133
Crete (S. Aegean)solar + wind + battery + green-H₂$140

Germany (typical)solar + wind + battery + green-H₂$168

Each sun + wind figure reflects the exact ERA5 grid cell at the site's coordinates, so very localized wind regimes (e.g. the Tarifa jet) can be under-captured — treat the ranking as directional. Geothermal/hydro costs are sourced to IRENA 2023 installed costs ($4,589/kW geothermal, $2,806/kW hydro) computed at the model's own WACC — just below IRENA's published LCOEs ($71 / $57); hydro uses CF 0.55 (reservoirs are energy-limited). From tools/build_eu_siting.py.

Key assumptions

AssumptionUSEuropeSource
Solar PV — LCOE₀ · learning rate$52/MWh · 30%$60/MWh · 30%Lazard v18; Way et al. Joule 2022
Onshore wind — LCOE₀ · LR$50/MWh · 17%$48/MWh · 17%Lazard v18; OWID
LFP battery — energy · power$180/kWh · $140/kW$180/kWh · $175/kWBloombergNEF 2024–25; Ember 2025
Gas price$4/MMBtu$10/MMBtuEIA Henry Hub / TTF forward
Carbon price trajectorylinear $0logistic $70→$200/tCO₂EU ETS Fit-for-55
WACC (solar/wind · battery · gas)5.5% · 7% · 9%5.5% · 7% · 9%NREL ATB 2024; merchant spread
Simulated capacity factor (solar / wind)0.23 / 0.330.16 / 0.29inside Lazard CF bands

All in real 2025 USD. Costs fall over time via Wright's-Law learning curves. Full derivations, data sources and the accuracy summary are in model_documentation.md.

What is and isn't modelled

  • Default = firm, always-on: gas backup covers 100% of load during lulls, so the worst case is a known, capped fuel cost. Premium/AI workloads never shed and collapse to this case.
  • Weather is synthetic but structured (multi-day Dunkelflaute, region-specific resource and sun–wind correlation). A real-weather seam (--weather, ERA5/NSRDB) and a multi-site portfolio (--sites) are built in but opt-in.
  • Single site by default — the largest directional caveat; a geographic portfolio softens the tails and lowers high-renewable cost.
  • Not modelled: sub-hourly load variation, on-site fuel logistics, transmission.
Model v5.7.0 · generated from output/*_firm_results.json at commit e029ca2 (config 592ffa0b20a21f50) · source on GitHub · licensed CC BY 4.0. Reproduce: make reproduce && make report.
